back: An entity set is a collection of entities of the same type that share the same attributes. It is a fundamental concept in the Entity-Relationship (ER) model that defines a group of objects (entities) with common properties.

front: Define the term 'Structural Constraint' in the context of ER modeling.
back: Structural constraints are rules that define the number of instances of one entity that can or must be associated with instances of another entity via a relationship set. The two main types are cardinality ratio and participation constraints.

front: What are the four possible Cardinality Ratios for a binary relationship?
back: The four cardinality ratios are: One-to-One (1:1), One-to-Many (1:N), Many-to-One (N:1), and Many-to-Many (M:N). They specify the maximum number of relationship instances an entity can participate in.

front: What is the difference between Total Participation and Partial Participation?
back: Total participation (existence dependency) means every entity in an entity set must participate in the relationship. Partial participation means that some entities in the set may not participate in the relationship. In an ER diagram, total participation is represented by a double line connecting the entity to the relationship.

front: How are key constraints and participation constraints different?
back: A key constraint (often shown as a cardinality ratio like 1:1, 1:N) specifies the *maximum* number of relationships an entity can have. A participation constraint (total or partial) specifies the *minimum* number of relationships an entity must have (either 0 for partial or 1 for total).
